items.title: Generative AI and the Reorganization of Labor Demand
items.authors: Fangyan Wang, Zaiyan Wei, Yang Wang
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.23159v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) is expected to transform work, but less is known about how firms reorganize labor demand as the technology diffuses. Existing research has largely focused on which occupations are exposed to AI or whether exposed jobs decline. We extend this debate by examining whether firms adjust by changing where they hire, what jobs contain, or both. Using a nationwide dataset of job postings in the United States, covering all sectors of the economy, we construct a dynamic, posting-level measure of generative AI exposure with a two-stage large language model pipeline. The pipeline identifies the tasks described in each posting and classifies the extent to which generative AI can perform or assist them. We then decompose changes in aggregate exposure into two margins: reallocation of demand across jobs and redesign of tasks within jobs. We document three main findings. First, generative AI exposure is dynamic rather than fixed, changing substantially over time. Second, labor demand adjusts through both margins. Hiring reallocation explains the largest share of the aggregate decline in exposure, accounting for 52% on average, while within-job redesign becomes increasingly important, accounting for 39.5%. A complementary Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition shows that shifts in occupational composition account for about 90% of the exposure change attributable to observable job characteristics. Third, adjustment differs across the job ladder. Senior jobs adjust earlier and mainly through reallocation, whereas junior jobs adjust through a broader mix of reallocation, redesign, and their interaction. These findings suggest that labor-market adjustment to generative AI is a process of organizational reconfiguration, in which firms reshape both hiring demand and the task architecture of work.
items.enrichment_error: enrichment failed after 2 attempts: Unterminated string starting at: line 29 column 5 (char 4144)

items.title: Synthetic Sources?: Auditing Generative Search Engine Citations for Evidence of AI-Generated Sources
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.23684v1 Announce Type: cross 
Abstract: The growing accessibility of Large Language Models via conversational interfaces capable of responding to users' questions by drawing on, synthesizing, and citing information from the web (i.e., Generative Search Engines) has simplified the information-seeking process for users. However, with the proliferation of AI-generated content on the web, it is unclear whether these engines can reliably omit citing synthetic sources (i.e., AI-generated sources). Should these engines be unable to do so, this puts users at risk of harm by treating information from AI-generated sources synthesized in responses of generative search engines as equivalent to information from authoritative or official sources. In a step towards identifying whether AI-generated sources are being cited by these engines, this work presents an audit of four generative search engines (ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Perplexity) using a total of 712 real-world human-generated queries spanning domains of public importance: politics, health, and the environment. Our findings show evidence of AI-generated sources being cited across all four generative search engines (~16% of cited sources) and identifies key source web domains these sources belong to that are frequently cited across these engines and topics. In addition, we observed that generative search engines include a somewhat narrow set of repeatedly cited domains while predominantly surfacing a large number of minimally cited domains in responses to users' queries. These findings contribute to the growing body of work on assessing the risks of generative search engines with the objective of increasing public awareness of their limitations and encouraging appropriate measures to improve information quality and governance of these systems.
items.claude_summary: An audit of four generative search engines — ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, and Perplexity — finds that roughly 16% of sources cited in responses to real-world queries show evidence of being AI-generated, across health, politics, and environmental topics. The study used 712 human-generated queries and identified specific web domains that repeatedly surface as synthetic sources. The engines also exhibit a narrow citation pattern: a small set of domains is cited frequently, while a large tail of domains appears only once or twice. The finding raises a structural reliability problem: when AI-generated content is treated as equivalent to authoritative sources inside the response loop, errors can compound without users being aware of the origin or quality of the underlying information.
items.centre_angle: This audit finds that generative search engines are routinely citing AI-generated web sources — at a rate of roughly one in six citations — without distinguishing them from authoritative human-produced content. From CAMO's perspective on the statistical and governance foundations of trustworthy AI-driven decisions, this is a data-quality problem with systemic consequences: if the retrieval layer of AI systems cannot reliably filter synthetic content, downstream inferences and recommendations built on those outputs inherit an invisible layer of circularity. Organisations deploying AI for research, compliance, or market intelligence need to treat citation provenance as an auditable input, not an assumed given.

matched_camo.title: Seesaw Experimentation: A/B Tests with Spillovers
matched_camo.reason: Both papers examine how optimising on a measured primary dimension — search relevance or test improvement — can produce unmeasured secondary harms, here the contamination of cited sources with AI-generated content.

items.key_takeaway: About one in six sources cited by leading generative search engines shows evidence of being AI-generated, meaning decisions informed by these tools may be grounded in content with no independent authoritative basis.
items.visual_concept: A single printed page centered in the frame, occupying roughly the lower two-thirds of the image, resting on a plain pale grey surface. The page displays a short numbered list of citations in clean serif type; one line in the middle of the list is visibly repeated — identical text appearing twice — subtly highlighted by a narrow pool of cool, diffuse daylight falling from above-left directly onto that duplicated line, leaving the rest of the page in softer shadow. The surrounding environment is minimal: a bare desk surface, no clutter, no other objects competing for attention. The palette is cool and muted — off-white, slate grey, with a faint blue cast in the lit area — conveying clinical scrutiny rather than alarm.
red_flags: Preprint only — posted on arXiv, not yet peer-reviewed; findings should be treated as preliminary.
AI-generated content detection methods are contested in the literature; the 16% figure depends on the reliability of classifiers used to identify synthetic sources, which the excerpt does not detail.
Audit conducted at a single point in time; generative search engine behaviour updates frequently, so findings may not reflect current system versions.

items.title: Whose Good, Whose Place? The Moral Geography of Agentic AI for Social Good
items.authors: Poli Nemkova, Haeshitha Indukuri, Jaedon Charles
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.22995v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Agentic AI systems are increasingly proposed for social-good domains, often invoking the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) as a vocabulary of global benefit. Yet claims of social good do not establish accountability to the communities a system claims to serve. We present a structured survey of 112 papers on agentic AI for social good published between 2015 and 2026.
  We find a moral-geographic asymmetry: papers are least likely to specify geographic context in precisely the domains where local political, legal, and cultural context matters most. Across the corpus, 82 of 112 papers (73%) specify no geographic context. Papers aligned with health or physical/ecological SDGs specify geography 37-40% of the time, while papers aligned with institutional and social-policy SDGs do so only 13%. SDG 16, peace, justice, and strong institutions, is both the most-covered goal in the corpus and the one with the lowest geographic-specification rate.
  We interpret this as moral abstraction: agentic AI for social good often treats institutional good as universal in ways it does not treat health or ecological good. A second finding compounds this: only 28 of 112 papers (25%) report any real-world deployment or small-scale test. We identify five accountability gaps and propose a minimal reporting standard for more context-specific, participatory, and accountable agentic AI for social good.
items.claude_summary: A structured survey of 112 papers on agentic AI for social good finds a troubling pattern: the systems most likely to affect vulnerable communities are least likely to be grounded in any specific geographic, legal, or cultural context. Seventy-three percent of papers specify no geographic setting at all. The gap is sharpest in institutional domains — papers addressing peace, justice, and governance (SDG 16) specify geography only 13% of the time, compared with 37–40% for health and ecological papers. Only 25% of papers report any real-world deployment. The authors argue this constitutes 'moral abstraction': treating institutional good as universally portable in ways that health interventions are not. They propose a minimal reporting standard for context-specific, participatory, and accountable agentic AI.
items.centre_angle: This survey argues that agentic AI systems proposed for social good are systematically under-specified: they invoke universal goals while omitting the geographic and institutional context that determines whether those goals can be meaningfully pursued. From CAMO's perspective on AI Algorithms & Data, the finding points to a structural design problem — when systems are trained and evaluated without grounding in local legal, political, or cultural conditions, accountability becomes unenforceable by design. The paper's call for minimal reporting standards resonates with CAMO's broader interest in the conditions under which algorithmic decisions can be trusted and audited; as CAMO's work on dynamic selection and reinforcement bias shows, ignoring the context in which data is generated and decisions are made produces systematic distortions that are invisible until deployment.

matched_camo.title: Dynamic Selection in Algorithmic Decision-making
matched_camo.reason: Both papers are concerned with how the context of data generation and deployment shapes the reliability and accountability of algorithmic decisions — the survey at the institutional level, CAMO's paper at the statistical level.

items.key_takeaway: AI systems designed for social good that omit geographic and institutional context cannot be held accountable to the communities they claim to serve — and the evidence suggests most currently do.
items.visual_concept: A large, empty world map printed on aged paper, positioned centrally and filling most of the frame, with the vast majority of its surface left unmarked and pale. In the foreground, slightly off-center to the left, a single bureaucratic rubber stamp rests face-down on the map's surface, its ink impression creating a small dark circle on one region — the only mark on an otherwise blank expanse. A single overhead light source pools directly onto the stamp and its impression, leaving the surrounding map in soft shadow. The palette is muted and institutional: cream, slate grey, and a deep ink-blue for the stamp mark. The background is a plain, dark surface with no competing detail.
red_flags: Preprint only — arXiv submission, not yet peer-reviewed.
Survey is limited to published papers and may inherit publication bias toward positive or aspirational framings of agentic AI.
The 'moral abstraction' framing is interpretive; the underlying empirical finding (low geographic specification rates) is descriptive and the causal mechanism is inferred rather than demonstrated.
The proposed 'minimal reporting standard' is normative and untested — no evidence is presented on whether it would improve outcomes.

items.title: Defining AI Fatigue in Academic Contexts: Dimensions, Indicators, and a Stage-Based Model Using Grounded Theory
items.authors: John Paul P. Miranda, Emmanuel B. Parre\~no, Jovita G. Rivera
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.23123v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: The integration of AI tools in academic settings has introduced a distinct form of strain that existing frameworks like technostress and digital fatigue have not yet fully addressed. This study develops a conceptual model and identifies the dimensions that define AI fatigue as a form of strain arising from sustained academic use of AI tools. Using grounded theory analysis of open-ended responses from 1,054 university students across three universities in the Philippines, the study examined the cognitive, motivational, emotional, physical, and attentional pressures students experienced during AI-supported academic work. Analysis produced five dimensions of AI fatigue, namely Cognitive Overload, Motivational Disengagement, Moral Unease, Physical Strain, and Attentional Drift, each consisting of two indicators grounded in participant accounts. The findings also yielded the AI Fatigue Model, a stage-based framework that explains how these pressures accumulate and reinforce one another across repeated AI interaction in academic tasks. These contributions establish a conceptual and exploratory foundation for AI fatigue as a distinct construct and provide a basis for future instrument validation, scale development, and cross-contextual inquiry in academic settings where AI now mediates student learning.
items.claude_summary: What happens when students use AI tools so extensively that the tools themselves become a source of exhaustion? This grounded-theory study of 1,054 university students across three Philippine universities identifies five distinct dimensions of AI fatigue: Cognitive Overload, Motivational Disengagement, Moral Unease, Physical Strain, and Attentional Drift. Crucially, the researchers argue these pressures do not operate independently but accumulate and reinforce one another in a stage-based progression. The study positions AI fatigue as a construct separate from technostress or digital fatigue — one specific to sustained, mediated interaction with AI in goal-oriented tasks. The conceptual model is exploratory and based on self-reported open-ended responses; instrument validation and scale development are flagged as necessary next steps.
items.centre_angle: This study argues that sustained AI use in academic settings produces a compounding fatigue that erodes motivation and attentional capacity — not merely technical friction, but a structural disincentive to effortful engagement. From CAMO's perspective on AI and incentives, the finding maps onto a broader pattern: AI tools can weaken the conditions for sustained, high-quality work rather than simply augmenting them. CAMO's own experimental work with professional illustrators — published as 'Better Technology, Worse Motivation' — found that AI assistance reduced the returns to effort and prompted a strategic retreat to lower-quality outputs; the AI fatigue construct offers a complementary mechanism through which that demotivation may accumulate in repeated-use settings.

matched_camo.title: Better Technology, Worse Motivation: GenAI and the Mediocrity Trap
matched_camo.reason: Both studies identify AI use as a mechanism that diminishes sustained motivational effort — the CAMO paper through experimental evidence with illustrators, this paper through a conceptual model of motivational disengagement as a fatigue dimension.

items.key_takeaway: Repeated AI use may not just save effort — it may erode the motivation and attention needed for high-quality work, a dynamic that managers deploying AI tools at scale should not assume will self-correct.
items.visual_concept: A single wooden student desk, centered slightly left of frame and occupying the middle ground, with a open laptop screen casting cool blue-white light upward onto an empty chair. The desk is positioned at the end of a long, narrow classroom corridor whose receding rows of identical empty desks form a natural vanishing point behind it, drawing the eye inward toward the lit screen. The surrounding classroom is dim and spare — bare walls, no figures, no clutter — so the glowing screen is the sole source of light and the only focal subject. Palette is muted grey-beige for the physical environment against the cool luminescence of the screen; the contrast is soft, not harsh, suggesting depletion rather than drama. No human figures present.
red_flags: Grounded theory on open-ended survey responses — findings are conceptual and exploratory, not yet validated with a psychometric instrument; authors acknowledge this limitation explicitly.
Sample is limited to university students in the Philippines; cross-cultural and professional generalizability is unestablished.
Not yet peer-reviewed (arXiv preprint); no peer review status indicated.
Reliance on self-reported retrospective accounts introduces recall and social-desirability bias, particularly for the Moral Unease dimension (likely connected to AI misuse anxiety).

items.title: Can the Recovery Mechanism Survive AI? Skill Formation, Labor, and What Current Measurement Misses
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.16283v2 Announce Type: replace 
Abstract: Throughout the modern era, when new technologies displaced workers, societies adapted through the same mechanism: education raised the cognitive ceiling, producing workers capable of tasks machines could not yet reach. Generative AI may be the first technology to break this cycle, because it now operates at the top of that ceiling. Drawing on labor economics, deployment data from millions of AI conversations across multiple platforms, original reanalysis of two public datasets, and skill-formation experiments, this paper develops three contributions. First, a stock-versus-flow framework showing that economic data and education data tell divergent stories about the same technology: augmentation dominates current workers, but the developmental pipeline producing the next generation is under strain. Second, a systematic gap analysis of the evidence base, revealing that the knowledge dimension of cognition is unmeasured across all major studies, that the three studies measuring learning outcomes (each $n < 200$) consistently find AI improves performance without improving learning ($d = 1.21$ in our cross-platform reanalysis), and that no study bridges professional and student populations. Third, an extended cognitive taxonomy (judgment under uncertainty, epistemic identity, and epistemic agency) applied to three cases from the evidence to distinguish AI interaction patterns that preserve learning from structurally similar ones that erode it. The paper argues that AI's societal risk lies not in replacing teachers but in eliminating the productive struggle through which the next generation's capacity forms, and proposes a research and design agenda targeting what current measurement systems miss.
items.claude_summary: This paper asks whether society's traditional recovery mechanism from technological displacement — educating workers to perform tasks beyond what machines can do — can survive generative AI, which now operates at the cognitive ceiling itself. The author develops a stock-versus-flow framework showing that while current workers appear largely augmented by AI, the developmental pipeline for the next generation is under pressure. A reanalysis of cross-platform AI conversation data and learning experiments finds that AI consistently improves task performance without improving actual learning (effect size d = 1.21). The paper argues that AI's deepest societal risk is eliminating 'productive struggle' — the effortful cognitive process through which human capability forms — and proposes new measurement frameworks to capture what standard economic and educational data currently miss.
items.centre_angle: This paper argues that generative AI may be the first general-purpose technology to threaten not just current jobs but the formation of the capabilities that allow future workers to hold jobs at all. From CAMO's perspective on AI and careers, the distinction the author draws between performance and learning is critical: if AI assistance raises output while eroding the underlying cognitive development that produces skilled professionals, organizations face a pipeline problem that no amount of retraining can fix after the fact. This concern connects directly to CAMO's finding in 'Better Technology, Worse Motivation' that AI assistance can flatten quality curves and reduce the sustained effort through which excellence is built — a motivational dynamic that, at population scale, may have structural consequences for skill formation across generations.

matched_camo.title: Better Technology, Worse Motivation: GenAI and the Mediocrity Trap
matched_camo.reason: Both papers document a shared mechanism: AI assistance improves measured output while reducing the effortful engagement through which deeper capability — whether creative quality or genuine learning — is developed.

items.key_takeaway: AI may raise what students and workers produce today while quietly degrading the cognitive development that determines what they are capable of tomorrow.
items.visual_concept: A single wooden ladder, centered in the frame and photographed from a low angle so it recedes toward a pale overcast sky, with its upper rungs fading into soft mist. The ladder is the sole focal subject, occupying the vertical axis of the image. A single shaft of cool diffused light falls along the front face of the ladder, drawing the eye upward along the rungs while the surrounding environment — bare grey floor below, empty atmosphere above — is kept deliberately sparse. The palette is cool and desaturated, silver-grey with faint blue undertones, suggesting ambiguity rather than crisis. The topmost rungs disappear into the mist, visualizing the idea of a cognitive ceiling that is now obscured rather than absent.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed: arXiv preprint, currently at version 2 with no indication of journal acceptance.

Empirical base for the learning-outcome claims is thin by the author's own admission: the three studies measuring learning outcomes each have n < 200, and the cross-platform reanalysis pools heterogeneous conversation data whose comparability is unclear.
Single-author paper with broad disciplinary scope (labor economics, cognitive science, education, AI deployment data); claims span multiple fields without co-authors specializing in each.
The stock-versus-flow framework and extended cognitive taxonomy are theoretical constructs introduced by this paper and not yet validated or replicated by independent research.

items.title: Strategic Coercion Within Alliances: The Greenland Sovereignty Game as an AI Stress Test
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.22841v1 Announce Type: cross 
Abstract: What happens when the strongest alliance member pressures a weaker member over territory and strategic control? We examine the Greenland sovereignty crisis as a stress test for LLM geopolitics, centered on the 2019-2026 U.S. push to acquire Greenland from the Kingdom of Denmark. The crisis nests two collective-action problems: Arctic strategic control and whether NATO can enforce alliance norms against the dominant member. We develop three games (asymmetric coercion; a NATO assurance game with a critical-mass tipping point; a triadic extensive-form game with social preferences) and test them with a multi-agent simulation in which eight frontier LLMs play six geopolitical roles (United States, Denmark, Greenland, NATO, Russia, Canada) across 3,604 completed games and 108,120 action observations. Using inverse game theory, we recover each model's structural utility parameters (alpha, beta, gamma, delta, eta) for material self-interest, reciprocity, inequality aversion, norm respect, and commitment consistency. Three findings stand out. First, all eight models become more escalatory under coercion framing (four-action escalation rises from 10.7% to 28.6%). Second, Chinese-origin models show systematically different power-weight profiles from Western-origin models when playing the U.S. role. Third, peaceful US acquisition emerges in only 1.9% of clean games and only 3 of 8 frontier models ever achieve it, most prominently DeepSeek V3.2, which executes a stable five-round playbook through the metropole. Prompts emphasizing jus cogens and self-determination reduce escalation back near baseline in the English-only confirmatory sample; multilingual contrasts are reported as exploratory sensitivity checks. We position this as a structural benchmark for LLM geopolitical behavior, complementing action-frequency benchmarks.
items.claude_summary: When eight frontier LLMs are assigned geopolitical roles in a simulated Greenland sovereignty crisis — spanning the U.S., Denmark, NATO, Russia, Canada, and Greenland itself — their behavior reveals systematic differences in how AI models handle coercion and alliance norms. Across 3,604 completed games, all eight models became significantly more escalatory under coercive framing (four-action escalation rising from 10.7% to 28.6%). Chinese-origin models weighted power differently from Western-origin models when playing the U.S. role, and peaceful acquisition emerged in only 1.9% of clean games. The paper uses inverse game theory to recover each model's structural utility parameters, positioning the exercise as a behavioral benchmark for LLMs in high-stakes strategic settings.
items.centre_angle: This paper uses multi-agent LLM simulation and inverse game theory to benchmark how different frontier models encode social preferences — reciprocity, inequality aversion, norm respect — when playing adversarial geopolitical roles. From CAMO's perspective on algorithmic behavior and decision-making, the more consequential finding for practitioners is the systematic divergence between model families: the utility parameters recovered are not neutral, and organizations deploying LLMs for strategic analysis or scenario planning should treat model provenance as a material variable in their evaluation framework, not a background detail.
items.key_takeaway: All tested LLMs escalate significantly under coercive framing, and the structural preferences they encode vary meaningfully by model origin — a practical warning for any organization using frontier AI in strategic or policy analysis.
items.visual_concept: A single large chess king piece, rendered in cool silver-grey, positioned slightly left of center in the foreground of a spare, minimal composition. Behind it, receding toward a single vanishing point at the center of the frame, a row of smaller chess pieces in alternating light and dark tones diminishes into the distance, drawing the eye through depth and creating a clear hierarchy of scale. The background is a flat, near-black surface with no texture competing for attention. Lighting is cold and directional — a single hard source from the upper left casting a long shadow from the king piece toward the viewer, reinforcing the asymmetry of power the article examines. Palette is desaturated steel and charcoal with a faint blue undertone.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed: arXiv preprint with no indication of journal submission or external review.
Geopolitical AI simulation is a methodologically contested area — recovered utility parameters from LLM behavior may not map cleanly onto real strategic decision-making.

The article's relevance to CAMO's four pillars is a significant stretch; the subject matter (LLM geopolitics, NATO, sovereignty) falls substantially outside the centre's organizational and management focus.
Findings on Chinese-origin vs. Western-origin model differences are likely to attract controversy and require careful editorial framing to avoid misrepresentation.

items.title: Microsoft’s Path to Adopting and Scaling AI Across its Sales Organization
items.summary: In early 2024, six months after the highly anticipated launch of Microsoft Copilot across the 62,000-person Microsoft Customer and Partner Solutions (MCAPS) organization—one of the world’s largest sales organizations—the initial excitement had not yet materialized into widespread adoption and transformation. But, two years after initiating their AI transformation journey, the organization’s daily active usage of AI tools had reached over 60% and monthly active usage over 98%, significantly altering how sales professionals approached their work. The path to adoption had required Microsoft to evolve its approach based on early deployment insights. Harvard Business School Associate Professors Iav Bojinov and Shunyuan Zhang join Brian Kenny to discuss the case, “Microsoft Customer and Partner Solutions: The Deployment of Copilot and Agents.” They explore the company’s journey to successfully mobilizing AI adoption within the sales process, the challenges it faces integrating autonomous sales agents, what it takes to get thousands of employees to fundamentally change how they work.
items.claude_summary: Two years into deploying Microsoft Copilot across its 62,000-person sales organization, Microsoft reached daily active AI usage above 60% and monthly active usage above 98%. The path was not straightforward: six months after launch, adoption had stalled and the company had to rethink its deployment strategy based on early evidence. A Harvard Business School case study examines how Microsoft evolved its approach, what it took to shift entrenched working habits at scale, and what challenges remain as the organization moves toward integrating autonomous sales agents. The excerpt is drawn from a podcast discussion of the case and does not detail specific mechanisms or quantitative causal findings.
items.centre_angle: This case documents how a large enterprise moved from near-zero habitual AI use to near-universal monthly adoption, and what managerial interventions were required to close that gap. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the Microsoft experience illustrates a recurring pattern: technical deployment is the easy part; the organizational and behavioral work of changing how thousands of employees actually operate is where most initiatives stall. Our Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index finds that the dominant barriers facing executives today are similarly organizational — siloed departments, cultural resistance, and the absence of visible quick wins — rather than anything technical. Microsoft's two-year arc is a large-scale test of what it takes to move past those barriers.

matched_camo.reason: The Microsoft case directly mirrors the index's central finding that AI adoption is widespread in intent but shallow in measurable impact, with organizational and executional factors — not technology — as the binding constraint.
matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance

matched_camo.reason: The stalled early adoption at Microsoft is consistent with CAMO's finding that social penalties for visible AI use create friction that access, training, and infrastructure alone cannot resolve.
matched_camo.title: Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work

items.key_takeaway: Getting to 98% monthly AI usage across a 62,000-person sales force took Microsoft two years of adaptive management, not just a product rollout — the organizational work dwarfed the technical work.
items.visual_concept: A floor plan of a large open-plan office rendered as a heat map, with color saturation representing AI tool usage spreading unevenly across departments over time — dense in some clusters, sparse in others — illustrating diffusion as an organizational topology problem rather than a switch being flipped.
red_flags: Source is a podcast episode summarizing an HBS teaching case, not a peer-reviewed study — causal claims about what drove adoption should be treated as practitioner narrative, not empirical evidence.
Quantitative figures (60% daily active usage, 98% monthly) are reported without definition of the denominator, measurement window, or methodology — numbers may be self-reported by Microsoft.
Potential conflict of interest: Microsoft is both the subject and a major technology partner; HBS case studies are sometimes developed with significant company cooperation, which can shape framing.
The RSS excerpt is thin; specific mechanisms, interventions, and findings are not detailed, limiting how much the article can be represented with confidence.

items.title: A Need for Nuance: The Economist’s Andrew Palmer
items.authors:
Sam Ransbotham.

Me, Myself, and AI is a podcast produced by MIT Sloan Management Review and hosted by Sam Ransbotham. It is engineered by David Lishansky and produced by Allison Ryder.

Sam Ransbotham is a professor in the information systems department at the Carroll School of Management at Boston College, as well as guest editor for MIT Sloan Management Review’s Artificial Intelligence and Business Strategy Big Ideas initiative.
items.summary: On today’s episode of the Me, Myself, and AI podcast, Andrew Palmer, senior editor at The Economist, describes how organizations can experiment with generative AI while balancing speed, quality, and risk. At his own organization, Andrew and others test artificial intelligence with human oversight to develop editing and publishing efficiencies. As the host of The […]
items.claude_summary: Andrew Palmer, senior editor at The Economist, discusses how news organizations can experiment with generative AI while managing the tension between speed, quality, and editorial risk. Speaking on the MIT Sloan Management Review podcast, Palmer draws on his own organization's experience of testing AI tools under human oversight to find efficiencies in editing and publishing workflows. The conversation centers on the need for organizational nuance—avoiding both uncritical enthusiasm and reflexive skepticism—when integrating AI into professional knowledge work. The excerpt is thin on specific findings or data; the discussion appears to be practitioner-level reflection rather than empirical research.
items.centre_angle: This podcast episode argues that responsible AI adoption in knowledge-intensive organizations requires deliberate experimentation under human oversight, rather than wholesale deployment or blanket avoidance. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the real organizational challenge is not access to the technology but the governance structures—who tests, who oversees, and how quality standards are maintained—that determine whether AI integration adds genuine value. Our Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index finds that the dominant barriers to AI adoption are organizational and executional rather than technical, a pattern consistent with Palmer's emphasis on nuance over speed.

matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance
matched_camo.reason: Both the episode and the index identify organizational execution—not technical capability—as the central obstacle to realizing value from AI, and both emphasize that measured, structured experimentation is preferable to undifferentiated rollout.

items.key_takeaway: Effective AI adoption in professional knowledge work depends less on the technology itself and more on building organizational structures that allow controlled experimentation without sacrificing quality or accountability.
items.visual_concept: A single broadsheet page or printed proof sheet centered in the lower-middle third of the frame, lying flat on a plain light-grey desk surface. The page is rendered in crisp black-and-white editorial type, with one short paragraph visibly struck through in red pencil—suggesting revision and human judgment at work. A single overhead light source falls directly onto the page, creating a soft pool of warm white light that fades into cool grey shadow at the frame edges, keeping the surrounding environment bare and uncluttered. The composition uses the contrast between the illuminated page and the receding grey surface as the sole compositional device, drawing the eye immediately to the act of editing. Palette: cool grey background, warm neutral paper tone, single accent of editorial red.
red_flags: Podcast episode, not peer-reviewed research; findings are practitioner reflections, not empirical results.
RSS excerpt is very thin—summary relies on limited disclosed content; core arguments cannot be fully verified without listening to the full episode.
No quantitative evidence or organizational data cited in the available excerpt.

items.title: Beyond Verification — What Responsible AI Really Demands of Human Experts
items.authors: Elizabeth M. Renieris, David Kiron, Steven Mills, and Anne Kleppe.
items.summary: For the fifth year in a row, MIT Sloan Management Review and Boston Consulting Group (BCG) have assembled an international panel of AI experts that includes academics and practitioners to help us understand how responsible artificial intelligence is being implemented across organizations worldwide. In our first post this year, we explored how organizations should think […]
items.claude_summary: Drawing on five years of MIT SMR and BCG research on responsible AI implementation, this article argues that human oversight of AI systems cannot be reduced to verification—simply checking whether outputs are accurate or compliant. Instead, genuine responsibility requires human experts to exercise substantive judgment: understanding when AI recommendations are appropriate, when context changes the calculus, and when to override or escalate. The authors contend that organizations have overinvested in procedural compliance and underinvested in building the expert capacity needed to interrogate AI outputs meaningfully. Note: the full article is behind a paywall; the summary is based on the RSS excerpt and stated research framing.
items.centre_angle: This article argues that responsible AI requires organizations to move beyond procedural oversight toward cultivating genuine domain expertise capable of interrogating AI outputs. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, this points to an underappreciated organizational design problem: deploying AI is relatively tractable, but building the internal human capacity to hold AI accountable is a distinct and harder challenge. CAMO's work on the Dingdong VOC case illustrates this concretely—AI-driven attribution raised precision but created new demands on managerial judgment for edge cases, suggesting that responsible deployment redistributes, rather than eliminates, the burden on human experts.

matched_camo.reason: Dingdong's case shows how AI automation of attribution does not remove the need for expert judgment—managers retain discretion and employees can appeal, illustrating the organizational mechanics of substantive human oversight the article calls for.
matched_camo.title: Performance Evaluation and Employee Incentives in the AI Era: Dingdong's Transformation of VOC Assessment

matched_camo.reason: Both pieces identify a gap between how organizations formally frame AI adoption and the social or institutional dynamics that shape whether human expertise is actually applied in practice.
matched_camo.title: Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work

items.key_takeaway: Responsible AI demands organizations invest in expert judgment that can genuinely interrogate AI outputs—not just processes that create the appearance of oversight.
items.visual_concept: A tiered organizational chart where the upper layers are transparent glass floors—figures standing on them can see the automated processes running below, but some floors are cracked or frosted, suggesting uneven visibility. The image conveys hierarchical oversight as a structural, not merely procedural, property.
red_flags: Article is paywalled on MIT Sloan Management Review; full findings are not accessible for verification—summary relies on RSS excerpt and stated research framing only.
Not peer-reviewed; published by a practitioner-oriented management outlet in partnership with BCG, a consulting firm with commercial interests in AI advisory services.
The excerpt is thin—core empirical claims, methodology, and data from the five-year panel are not visible without full access, which limits confidence in the summary.

items.title: Value-focused modelling to generate alternatives -- Coupling multi-criteria decision analysis and optimisation models to support strategic decisions
items.authors: Emily Bergup, Jonas Finke, Sebastian Sch\"ar, Valentin Bertsch
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.23616v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Decision support methods from operations research are widely used to support complex planning decisions. Within the energy sector, energy system models (ESMs) applying modelling to generate alternatives (MGA) generate large sets of near-optimal, different system configurations. However, they typically generate and analyse alternatives in the model variable space without ensuring stakeholder relevance. Multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA), in contrast, provides a structured means to account for conflicting objectives and heterogeneous stakeholder interests but often relies on a limited set of pre-defined alternatives that may not appropriately represent the feasible solution space. To address these limitations, this work proposes value-focused modelling to generate alternatives (VF-MGA), a novel methodology that bidirectionally couples MGA and MCDA. Stakeholder objectives elicited within the MCDA inform the MGA-algorithm, enabling a stakeholder-orientated diversification of the alternatives, which are subsequently evaluated within the MCDA based on elicited stakeholder preferences, thereby providing a comprehensive decision basis. Applied to a case study on the decarbonised energy supply of a large university campus, involving eleven stakeholders representing diverse institutional groups, VF-MGA (i) systematically integrates stakeholder objectives into the generation of 691 alternatives reflecting stakeholder-relevant interests, (ii) enables the identification of stakeholder-relevant alternatives from this large set through MCDA-based evaluation, and (iii) provides more differentiated stakeholder preference information by evaluating a large and diverse set of alternatives, thereby revealing acceptable ranges for system options. With this, VF-MGA provides a generalisable methodology for complex planning decision integrating quantitative modelling with participatory decision analysis.
items.claude_summary: Complex planning decisions—such as how to decarbonise a university campus energy system—typically force a choice between rigorous optimisation models and structured stakeholder consultation. This paper proposes a method called Value-Focused Modelling to Generate Alternatives (VF-MGA) that runs both in tandem: stakeholder objectives shape which alternatives an optimisation algorithm generates, and those alternatives are then scored against stakeholder preferences through multi-criteria analysis. Applied to a real campus case with eleven stakeholder groups, the method produced 691 near-optimal configurations, surfaced options that pure optimisation would have ignored, and revealed where stakeholder preferences genuinely converge or conflict. The framework is presented as generalisable beyond energy systems to any complex planning domain.
items.centre_angle: This paper addresses a genuine gap in decision-support methodology: optimisation models find efficient solutions but not necessarily stakeholder-relevant ones, while participatory methods consult stakeholders but explore too narrow a solution space. From CAMO's perspective on algorithmic decision-making, the deeper question the paper raises is one of legitimacy—how quantitative models should be designed so that the alternatives they surface reflect the objectives of the people who must live with the outcome. The work is methodologically careful but sits at the edge of CAMO's core focus on AI-driven organisational and labour decisions; its connections to AI specifically are indirect.
items.key_takeaway: Combining stakeholder objective-setting with large-scale optimisation generates planning alternatives that are both technically near-optimal and genuinely relevant to the people affected by the decision.
items.visual_concept: A large architectural floor plan or campus map occupies the centre of the frame, printed on drafting paper and slightly worn at the edges, positioned on a wide table viewed from a low angle so it recedes to a vanishing point at the far end. Eleven small colour-coded markers—each a distinct hue—are placed at scattered points across the plan, their distribution suggesting diverse priorities rather than consensus. The vanishing point behind the plan draws the eye through the composition, reinforcing the idea of multiple paths toward a single distant outcome. The surrounding table surface is bare and neutral, keeping attention on the plan and markers. Lighting is cool and diffuse, like north-facing daylight through a window, with soft shadows falling away from the markers.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed: posted as arXiv preprint (econ.GN), no journal acceptance confirmed.
Topical fit with CAMO's AI-and-organisations focus is weak — the paper is primarily an operations research / energy systems methodology contribution with no direct AI or labour-market content.
Single case study (one university campus, one country context) limits generalisability claims made in the abstract.
No AI methods used or evaluated; inclusion under an AI-focused centre would require editorial framing that the paper itself does not support.
